subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Think for a moment about a normal evening in your home. The sun has gone down over Mogadishu, Hargeisa, or Nairobi, and the sky is dark. Then someone flicks a small switch on the wall, and suddenly the whole room fills with light. A torch helps your family find their way to the well at night. A phone charger fills up a battery so you can call your grandmother. A small radio plays the news while your family shares bariis and goat meat for dinner. Every one of these useful things is powered by the same amazing invisible energy: electricity.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the Stage 4 science workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: By the end of this unit, you will be able to do all of these things. Tick each one off as you master it:
Explain that electricity is a form of energy that can flow through certain materials.
Name the main parts of a simple circuit (cell, wires, bulb, switch) and say what each one does.
Build a complete circuit that lights a small bulb, and explain why a broken circuit does not work.
Sort everyday materials into conductors and insulators by testing them.
Describe how a switch opens and closes a circuit to control electricity.
Explain how adding more cells makes a bulb brighter, and adding more bulbs in a line makes each one dimmer.
Recognise the difference between a series circuit and a parallel circuit.
Read and draw simple circuit symbols used by scientists everywhere.
Follow the golden rules of electrical safety and know when to fetch an adult.

concepts.title: In this unit, you will become an electricity detective. You…
concepts.explanation: In this unit, you will become an electricity detective. You will discover what electricity really is, how it travels along a hidden path called a circuit, and why some materials let it pass through while others block it completely. You will build real, working circuits with your own hands, using only safe 1.5-volt batteries, small bulbs, and wires. You will learn how a switch turns things on and off, how to make a bulb shine brighter or dimmer, and, most importantly of all, how to stay safe around electricity…
concepts.example: Electricity is one of the most important forms of energy in the whole world. In many parts of East Africa, families use a wonderful mix of power: solar panels on the roof that catch the strong equatorial sun, batteries…

concepts.title: Electricity is one of the most important forms of energy in…
concepts.explanation: Electricity is one of the most important forms of energy in the whole world. In many parts of East Africa, families use a wonderful mix of power: solar panels on the roof that catch the strong equatorial sun, batteries that store energy for the night, torches and battery radios, and mains electricity from the wires that come into towns and cities. Understanding electricity helps you understand the modern world around you. It also helps you use energy wisely and, above all, safely. Let us begin our investigation.
concepts.example: As you work through this unit, remember that a scientist learns best by doing. Do not just read about circuits; build them. Do not just read about conductors; test real objects from your own home. Every time you make a…

concepts.title: As you work through this unit, remember that a scientist…
concepts.explanation: As you work through this unit, remember that a scientist learns best by doing. Do not just read about circuits; build them. Do not just read about conductors; test real objects from your own home. Every time you make a prediction and then check it, you are training your mind to think like a true investigator. Keep a science journal beside you, write down your questions, and use your AI tutor whenever something puzzles you. By the end of this unit, electricity will no longer be a mystery hidden inside the wall. It…
concepts.example: [*] Did You Know? The very first electric light bulbs were invented in the 1870s, less than 150 years ago. Before that, people everywhere used oil lamps, candles, and firelight after dark. Today, solar panels let a…

concepts.title: [*] Did You Know? The very first electric light bulbs were…
concepts.explanation: [*] Did You Know? The very first electric light bulbs were invented in the 1870s, less than 150 years ago. Before that, people everywhere used oil lamps, candles, and firelight after dark. Today, solar panels let a family in a village with no power lines still charge a phone and light a room from sunlight alone.

explorations.title: Build Your First Circuit
explorations.context: To build a complete circuit that lights a small bulb, and to discover what happens when the loop is broken. Make a Prediction Before you start, write down: What do you think you must do to make the bulb light up? What do you think will happen if you leave a…
explorations.prompt: What three parts did your working circuit need?
explorations.answer: What three parts did your working circuit need? What happened when you made a gap in the loop? Why? Was your prediction correct? What did you learn?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To build a complete circuit that lights a small bulb, and to discover what happens when the loop is broken. Make a Prediction Before you start, write down: What do you think you must do to make the bulb light up? What do you think will happen if you leave a…

explorations.title: Conductor or Insulator?
explorations.context: To test everyday objects from around your home and sort them into conductors (let electricity through) and insulators (block electricity). Make a Prediction Look at each object you will test. Predict whether you think it is a conductor or an insulator. Write…
explorations.prompt: Which objects were conductors? What do most of them have in common?
explorations.answer: Which objects were conductors? What do most of them have in common? Which objects were insulators? What are they made of? Were any of your predictions wrong? Which ones surprised you? Why do you think electrical wires are made of metal but covered in plastic?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To test everyday objects from around your home and sort them into conductors (let electricity through) and insulators (block electricity). Make a Prediction Look at each object you will test. Predict whether you think it is a conductor or an insulator. Write…

explorations.title: Make Your Own Switch
explorations.context: To build a working switch that turns your bulb on and off by completing and breaking the circuit. Make a Prediction Predict: how will pressing two metal paper clips together turn your bulb on?
explorations.prompt: When is your switch 'on' (closed): when the clips touch, or when they are apart?
explorations.answer: When is your switch 'on' (closed): when the clips touch, or when they are apart? Why does the bulb go out when you open the switch? Where in your home can you find switches that work in a similar way?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To build a working switch that turns your bulb on and off by completing and breaking the circuit. Make a Prediction Predict: how will pressing two metal paper clips together turn your bulb on?

explorations.title: Brighter and Dimmer
explorations.context: To investigate how changing the number of cells and the number of bulbs changes the brightness of the light. Make a Prediction Predict: What will happen to the brightness if you (a) add a second battery, and (b) add a second bulb in a line?
explorations.prompt: What happened to the brightness when you added a second battery? Why?
explorations.answer: What happened to the brightness when you added a second battery? Why? What happened when you added a second bulb in the line? Why do they share the push? In the two-bulb circuit, what do you predict will happen to the second bulb if you remove the first? Try it.
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To investigate how changing the number of cells and the number of bulbs changes the brightness of the light. Make a Prediction Predict: What will happen to the brightness if you (a) add a second battery, and (b) add a second bulb in a line?

visualModels.purpose: By the end of this unit, you will be able to do all of these things. Tick each one off as you master it:

methods.title: Build Your First Circuit
methods.example: To build a complete circuit that lights a small bulb, and to discover what happens when the loop is broken. Make a Prediction Before you start, write down: What do you think you must do to make the bulb light up? What do you think will happen if you leave a…
steps: Tape one bared wire end firmly to the flat (negative) end of the battery.
Tape one bared wire end firmly to the bumpy (positive) end of the battery.
Touch the free end of the first wire to the metal base of the bulb.
Touch the free end of the second wire to the metal side of the bulb.
Observe: does the bulb light up? You have made a complete circuit!

methods.title: Conductor or Insulator?
methods.example: To test everyday objects from around your home and sort them into conductors (let electricity through) and insulators (block electricity). Make a Prediction Look at each object you will test. Predict whether you think it is a conductor or an insulator. Write…
steps: Set up your circuit so the bulb lights only when the two free wire ends touch each other.
Now touch the two free ends onto the object you are testing (one end on each side of the object).
If the bulb lights, electricity is flowing through the object: it is a conductor.
If the bulb stays dark, the object is blocking electricity: it is an insulator.
Record your result, then test the next object. Keep everything dry.

methods.title: Make Your Own Switch
methods.example: To build a working switch that turns your bulb on and off by completing and breaking the circuit. Make a Prediction Predict: how will pressing two metal paper clips together turn your bulb on?
steps: Cut one of the wires in your circuit so there is now a gap. The bulb will be off.
Tape one metal paper clip to the cardboard, connected to one free wire end.
Tape the second paper clip next to it, connected to the other free wire end, but with a small gap between the two clips.
Press the two paper clips together so they touch. Observe the bulb.
Lift them apart again. Observe the bulb. You have made a switch!

methods.title: Brighter and Dimmer
methods.example: To investigate how changing the number of cells and the number of bulbs changes the brightness of the light. Make a Prediction Predict: What will happen to the brightness if you (a) add a second battery, and (b) add a second bulb in a line?
steps: Build a simple circuit with one battery and one bulb. Note how bright the bulb is.
Add a second battery in a line with the first. Note the brightness now.
Go back to one battery. Now add a second bulb in the same single loop (in series).
Note how bright each bulb is now compared to a single bulb.
Recording Your Results

workedExamples.title: Making a Bulb Brighter
workedExamples.prompt: Let us work through a real problem together, step by step, just as a scientist would. This shows you how to think your way to an answer.
workedExamples.solution: Problem: Yusuf has built a simple circuit with one 1.5V cell and one small bulb. The bulb glows, but only faintly. He wants it to shine more brightly. What can he do, and what must he be careful about? Step 1 - Understand the cause. The brightness of a bulb depends on how much current flows through it, and the current depends on the electrical push, the voltage, from the cell. One cell gives a fairly small push, so the bulb is dim. Step 2 - Choose a change. To increase the push, Yusuf can add a second cell in a…

workedExamples.title: Guided example 2
workedExamples.prompt: A complete circuit must be: (a) a straight line (b) an unbroken loop (c) made of plastic (d) very long
workedExamples.solution: 2 (b) an unbroken loop Current only flows if the path is a complete loop with no gaps.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 3
workedExamples.prompt: Which of these is a conductor? (a) plastic spoon (b) rubber band (c) steel nail (d) dry wood
workedExamples.solution: 3 (c) steel nail Steel is a metal, and most metals are good conductors.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 4
workedExamples.prompt: Which of these is an insulator? (a) copper wire (b) aluminium foil (c) a coin (d) a plastic ruler
workedExamples.solution: 4 (d) plastic ruler Plastic blocks electricity, so it is an insulator.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: A switch is used to: (a) make electricity (b) turn a circuit on and off (c) store energy (d) cool the bulb
workedExamples.solution: 5 (b) turn a circuit on and off A switch opens (off) or closes (on) the loop.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: For school experiments we should only use: (a) wall sockets (b) 1.5V batteries (c) power lines (d) car batteries
workedExamples.solution: 6 (b) 1.5V batteries Only low-voltage batteries are safe. Mains and car batteries are dangerous.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: If you add a second battery in a line, the bulb will usually get: (a) dimmer (b) brighter (c) colder (d) stay the same
workedExamples.solution: 7 (b) brighter More cells give a stronger push (voltage), so more current flows and the bulb is brighter.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: In a series circuit with two bulbs, each bulb is: (a) brighter than one bulb (b) dimmer than one bulb (c) off (d) a different colour
workedExamples.solution: 8 (b) dimmer than one bulb Two bulbs in series share the push from one cell, so each is dimmer.

practice.prompt: Electricity is a kind of: (a) water (b) energy (c) metal (d) light only
practice.answer: 1 (b) energy Electricity is a form of energy that can flow and change into light, heat, movement, and sound.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A complete circuit must be: (a) a straight line (b) an unbroken loop (c) made of plastic (d) very long
practice.answer: 2 (b) an unbroken loop Current only flows if the path is a complete loop with no gaps.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these is a conductor? (a) plastic spoon (b) rubber band (c) steel nail (d) dry wood
practice.answer: 3 (c) steel nail Steel is a metal, and most metals are good conductors.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these is an insulator? (a) copper wire (b) aluminium foil (c) a coin (d) a plastic ruler
practice.answer: 4 (d) plastic ruler Plastic blocks electricity, so it is an insulator.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A switch is used to: (a) make electricity (b) turn a circuit on and off (c) store energy (d) cool the bulb
practice.answer: 5 (b) turn a circuit on and off A switch opens (off) or closes (on) the loop.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: For school experiments we should only use: (a) wall sockets (b) 1.5V batteries (c) power lines (d) car batteries
practice.answer: 6 (b) 1.5V batteries Only low-voltage batteries are safe. Mains and car batteries are dangerous.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: If you add a second battery in a line, the bulb will usually get: (a) dimmer (b) brighter (c) colder (d) stay the same
practice.answer: 7 (b) brighter More cells give a stronger push (voltage), so more current flows and the bulb is brighter.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: In a series circuit with two bulbs, each bulb is: (a) brighter than one bulb (b) dimmer than one bulb (c) off (d) a different colour
practice.answer: 8 (b) dimmer than one bulb Two bulbs in series share the push from one cell, so each is dimmer.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: What part turns electrical energy into light? (a) the wire (b) the switch (c) the bulb (d) the battery
practice.answer: 9 (c) the bulb A bulb changes electrical energy into light energy.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which is the safest thing to do if you see a fallen power line? (a) touch it (b) move it (c) stay far away and tell an adult (d) pour water on it
practice.answer: 10 (c) stay far away and tell an adult A fallen line can be deadly. Never touch it; get an adult at once.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Why is water dangerous near electricity? (a) it is cold (b) it can conduct electricity (c) it is an insulator (d) it makes light
practice.answer: 11 (b) it can conduct electricity Water, especially with salt or dirt, lets electricity flow, causing shocks.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The bumpy end of a battery is the: (a) negative end (b) positive end (c) switch (d) insulator
practice.answer: 12 (b) positive end The bumpy end of a battery is the positive (+) end.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: Build Your First Circuit
activities.materials: One 1.5V battery (AA or AAA), or two joined together; A small 1.5V torch bulb (or an LED from an old toy); Two pieces of insulated wire (thin copper wire from an old cable works; ask an adult to bare the ends); Sticky tape to hold the wires onto the battery ends
steps: Tape one bared wire end firmly to the flat (negative) end of the battery.
Tape one bared wire end firmly to the bumpy (positive) end of the battery.
Touch the free end of the first wire to the metal base of the bulb.
Touch the free end of the second wire to the metal side of the bulb.
Observe: does the bulb light up? You have made a complete circuit!

activities.title: Conductor or Insulator?
activities.materials: Your working circuit from Experiment 1, but leave a small gap by adding a third short wire, so you have two free ends to touch objects with; A collection of safe, dry household objects: a metal spoon, a plastic spoon, a steel nail, a rubber band, a coin, a piece of dry wood, aluminium foil, a glass…
steps: Set up your circuit so the bulb lights only when the two free wire ends touch each other.
Now touch the two free ends onto the object you are testing (one end on each side of the object).
If the bulb lights, electricity is flowing through the object: it is a conductor.
If the bulb stays dark, the object is blocking electricity: it is an insulator.
Record your result, then test the next object. Keep everything dry.

activities.title: Make Your Own Switch
activities.materials: Your circuit from Experiment 1; Two metal paper clips (or two brass paper fasteners); A small piece of stiff cardboard; Sticky tape
steps: Cut one of the wires in your circuit so there is now a gap. The bulb will be off.
Tape one metal paper clip to the cardboard, connected to one free wire end.
Tape the second paper clip next to it, connected to the other free wire end, but with a small gap between the two clips.
Press the two paper clips together so they touch. Observe the bulb.
Lift them apart again. Observe the bulb. You have made a switch!

activities.title: Brighter and Dimmer
activities.materials: Two 1.5V batteries; Two identical small bulbs; Wires and tape
steps: Build a simple circuit with one battery and one bulb. Note how bright the bulb is.
Add a second battery in a line with the first. Note the brightness now.
Go back to one battery. Now add a second bulb in the same single loop (in series).
Note how bright each bulb is now compared to a single bulb.
Recording Your Results

terms: Electricity
A form of energy that flows through some materials and makes things work.
Circuit
A complete, unbroken loop that electricity can flow around.
Cell
A single store of electrical energy (everyday name: battery).
Battery
One or more cells joined to store more energy.
Current
The flow of electricity around a circuit.
Conductor
A material that lets electricity flow through it (most metals).
Insulator
A material that blocks electricity (plastic, rubber, wood, glass).
Switch
A part that opens or closes a circuit to turn it on or off.
Bulb
A part that changes electrical energy into light energy.
Series circuit
A circuit with a single loop; all parts share one path.
Voltage
The strength of the electrical push. Batteries are low/safe; mains is high/dangerous.
Solar panel
A device that turns sunlight into electrical energy.
commonMistakes: A bulb lights with one wire.
There is no return path, so the loop has a gap.
Batteries and sockets are equally safe.
Mains voltage is hundreds of times stronger than a battery.
Current is used up in the first bulb.
The same current flows all the way round a series loop.
Too many cells burn out the bulb's filament.

realProblems.prompt: Use what you know to solve these real-life problems.
realProblems.answer: Check: (1) are the batteries fresh and the right way round? (2) is the bulb loose or burned out? (3) is any wire or connection loose, making a gap in the loop?
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Check: (1) are the batteries fresh and the right way round? (2) is the bulb loose or burned out? (3) is any wire or connection loose, making a gap in the loop?

realProblems.prompt: Farah's torch will not light. List three things he should check to find the problem.
realProblems.answer: In the day, sunlight falls on the solar panel, which turns light energy into electrical energy. This energy is stored in a battery. At night, the stored energy flows from the battery to the bulb, giving light.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: In the day, sunlight falls on the solar panel, which turns light energy into electrical energy. This energy is stored in a battery. At night, the stored energy flows from the battery to the bulb, giving light.

realProblems.prompt: Halima's family uses a solar panel and a battery. Explain, in simple steps, how sunlight in the day gives them light at night.
realProblems.answer: Omar puts the key into a gap in his battery circuit, touching a wire to each side of the key. If the bulb lights, current is flowing through the key, proving it is a conductor.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Omar puts the key into a gap in his battery circuit, touching a wire to each side of the key. If the bulb lights, current is flowing through the key, proving it is a conductor.

realProblems.prompt: Omar wants to test if his metal key is a conductor. Describe how he could use a battery circuit to find out, and what result would prove it is a conductor.
realProblems.answer: Old batteries give a weaker push (lower voltage), so less current flows to the radio, and the sound becomes weak.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Old batteries give a weaker push (lower voltage), so less current flows to the radio, and the sound becomes weak.

realProblems.prompt: A radio in Hargeisa runs on two batteries. When the batteries get old, the sound becomes weak. Explain why, using the idea of electrical push.
realProblems.answer: She should not touch it, keep away, and tell a trusted adult. Bare metal is dangerous because you could touch the conductor and get a shock; the insulating cover is meant to keep the electricity safely inside.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: She should not touch it, keep away, and tell a trusted adult. Bare metal is dangerous because you could touch the conductor and get a shock; the insulating cover is meant to keep the electricity safely inside.

realProblems.prompt: Zahra sees a cable in her home with the plastic cover torn and bare metal showing. What should she do, and why is this dangerous?
realProblems.answer: Work through the task and check with your teacher or the reference card.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Work through the task and check with your teacher or the reference card.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What three main parts does a simple circuit need?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A cell (or battery), wires, and a component such as a bulb. (A switch is often added too.)

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the difference between a complete circuit and a broken circuit.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A complete circuit is an unbroken loop, so current flows and the bulb lights. A broken circuit has a gap, so current cannot flow and the bulb stays dark.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What is a conductor? Give two examples.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A conductor lets electricity flow through it easily. Examples: copper wire, a steel nail, aluminium foil (any two metals).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What is an insulator? Give two examples.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: An insulator blocks electricity. Examples: plastic, rubber, dry wood, glass, paper (any two).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: How does a switch control the electricity in a circuit?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A switch makes or breaks a gap in the loop. Closed, it completes the loop and current flows; open, it breaks the loop and current stops.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Why must you only ever use batteries, and never wall sockets, for your experiments?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Because a 1.5V battery gives a small, safe push, but wall sockets give a very high, dangerous voltage that can badly injure you.

questions.question: Electricity is a kind of:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: energy Electricity is a form of energy that can flow and change into light, heat, movement, and sound.

questions.question: A complete circuit must be:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: an unbroken loop Current only flows if the path is a complete loop with no gaps.

questions.question: Which of these is a conductor?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: steel nail Steel is a metal, and most metals are good conductors.

questions.question: Which of these is an insulator?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: plastic ruler Plastic blocks electricity, so it is an insulator.

questions.question: A switch is used to:
options: turn a circuit on and off
questions.answer: turn a circuit on and off
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: turn a circuit on and off A switch opens (off) or closes (on) the loop.

questions.question: For school experiments we should only use:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: 1.5V batteries Only low-voltage batteries are safe. Mains and car batteries are dangerous.

questions.question: If you add a second battery in a line, the bulb will usually get:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: brighter More cells give a stronger push (voltage), so more current flows and the bulb is brighter.

questions.question: In a series circuit with two bulbs, each bulb is:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: dimmer than one bulb Two bulbs in series share the push from one cell, so each is dimmer.

questions.question: What part turns electrical energy into light?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: the bulb A bulb changes electrical energy into light energy.

questions.question: Which is the safest thing to do if you see a fallen power line?
options: stay far away and tell an adult
questions.answer: stay far away and tell an adult
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: stay far away and tell an adult A fallen line can be deadly. Never touch it; get an adult at once.

questions.question: Why is water dangerous near electricity?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: it can conduct electricity Water, especially with salt or dirt, lets electricity flow, causing shocks.

questions.question: The bumpy end of a battery is the:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: positive end The bumpy end of a battery is the positive (+) end.

selfAssessment: I can by the end of this unit, you will be able to do all of these things. Tick each one off as you master it:
I can explain that electricity is a form of energy that can flow through certain materials.
I can name the main parts of a simple circuit (cell, wires, bulb, switch) and say what each one does.
I can build a complete circuit that lights a small bulb, and explain why a broken circuit does not work.
I can sort everyday materials into conductors and insulators by testing them.
I can describe how a switch opens and closes a circuit to control electricity.
I can explain how adding more cells makes a bulb brighter, and adding more bulbs in a line makes each one dimmer.
I can recognise the difference between a series circuit and a parallel circuit.
